First state if you agree/disagree with prior conclusions, then write at most 2 sentences explaining why, ending with clear answer choice in boxed{} format.
Based on the retrieved evidence, VLEMs are not consistently tied to inflammation. While eosinophilic esophagitis can show furrowed or corrugated mucosa, there’s no explicit link established between VLEMs and active esophagitis. Instead, VLEMs resemble findings observed in structural abnormalities—such as those due to venous congestion—or post-procedure changes rather than being definitive markers of inflammatory disease.
raw_response:
Tool Call: retrieve("association between vertical lines in distal esophageal mucosa and esophagitis in children")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

First state if you agree/disagree with prior conclusions, then write at most 2 sentences explaining why, ending with clear answer choice in boxed{} format.
Based on the retrieved evidence, VLEMs are not consistently tied to inflammation. While eosinophilic esophagitis can show furrowed or corrugated mucosa, there’s no explicit link established between VLEMs and active esophagitis. Instead, VLEMs resemble findings observed in structural abnormalities—such as those due to venous congestion—or post-procedure changes rather than being definitive markers of inflammatory disease. Therefore, considering the lack of consistent association with classic signs of inflammation and the potential overlap with benign or mechanical alterations, I conclude that VLEMs do not reliably indicate esophagitis in children.
\boxed{\text{B}}
